question_title: Will Google make Gemini 3.5 Pro generally available before July 12, 2026?
summary: The question requires Gemini 3.5 Pro to reach full GA/stable status specifically on the Gemini API pages (not Vertex preview) between June 27 and July 11, 2026 — a tight ~11-day window. As of June 29, the model is still only in limited Vertex enterprise preview and entirely absent from the Gemini API docs. Google has already slipped the target once (June→July), won't commit to a date, and declined to comment. The resolution bar is high: even if Google holds an early-July launch event, its standard experimental→preview→GA progression means an initial Gemini API listing would most likely carry a -preview suffix, which the criteria explicitly exclude. Competitive and talent pressure provides some upside force, but multiple independent factors favor NO. Estimate ~10%.

factors.description: Window covers only first ~11 days of July; even if 'July 2026' GA lands uniformly in July, only ~35% of the month falls in window, and launches tend to cluster later or slip
Already slipped once (June->July); Google declined to comment and has no committed date, signaling continued uncertainty

Model is still only in limited Vertex enterprise preview, not even on the Gemini API pages; Google's typical path is experimental->preview->GA, so a near-term Gemini API release would most likely appear as -preview (excluded by criteria), not GA/stable
Strong public commitment and competitive/talent pressure (GPT-5.6, Claude Opus, researcher exodus to Anthropic) could push an early-July launch event; Flash went straight to availability on May 19

reasoning:
**Forecast: ~10% (NO favored)**

Google making Gemini 3.5 Pro generally available on the Gemini API pages before July 12, 2026 is unlikely. Key reasoning:

- **Tight window**: Only the first ~11 days of July, while the "July 2026" target is vague and uncommitted.
- **High bar**: Requires full GA/stable status specifically on the Gemini API changelog/models pages — Vertex preview explicitly doesn't count. As of June 29, the model isn't even listed there in preview form.
- **Track record**: Already slipped once (June→July), Google declined to comment, and no specific date is committed.
- **Preview-first pattern**: Google's typical experimental→preview→GA progression means even an early-July launch would most likely appear with a `-preview` suffix first, which the criteria exclude.
- **Counterweight**: Competitive pressure (GPT-5.6, Claude Opus) and the talent-exodus narrative give Google motivation to ship quickly — but not enough to overcome the structural constraints.

Final probability: **0.10**
sources_consulted: [Gemini 3.5 Pro Cleared for July Launch as Fable 5 Nears Return, GPT-5.6 Stays Locked](https://www.techtimes.com/articles/319318/20260629/gemini-35-pro-cleared-july-launch-fable-5-nears-return-gpt-56-stays-locked.htm)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, at any time between June 27, 2026 and 23:59 UTC on July 11, 2026, Google lists a "Gemini 3.5 Pro" model as generally available (GA / stable, as opposed to preview, experimental, or limited enterprise preview) on either the official Gemini API release notes (https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/changelog) or the Gemini API models documentation (https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/models). A model ID of the form "gemini-3.5-pro" (without a "-preview" or "-exp" suffix) appearing as an available, non-preview model on these pages, or an explicit changelog entry announcing Gemini 3.5 Pro reaching general availability/stable status, counts as YES. Otherwise the question resolves NO.
fine_print: "Generally available" means Google designates the model as GA or stable (not preview, experimental, early access, or limited/enterprise preview). A preview, experimental, or limited-access release of Gemini 3.5 Pro that has not reached GA/stable status does NOT count. Availability of the model only on Vertex AI or other surfaces, while not GA on the Gemini API pages above, does NOT count. If Google publishes the model under a slightly different name (e.g., "Gemini 3.5 Pro" with a model ID such as "gemini-3.5-pro" or a clearly equivalent stable identifier) and explicitly designates it GA/stable, that counts. If the listed primary pages are unavailable, an official Google AI or Google Cloud blog post explicitly announcing Gemini 3.5 Pro general availability, or reporting by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) of such an official announcement, may be used. The release must occur on or before July 11, 2026 (UTC) to count.

factors.description: Window covers only first ~11 days of July; even if 'July 2026' GA lands uniformly in July, only ~35% of the month falls in window, and launches tend to cluster later or slip
Already slipped once (June->July); Google declined to comment and has no committed date, signaling continued uncertainty

Model is still only in limited Vertex enterprise preview, not even on the Gemini API pages; Google's typical path is experimental->preview->GA, so a near-term Gemini API release would likely be -preview (excluded), not GA
Strong public commitment and competitive/talent pressure to ship in July could push a launch event in early July

input.anchor: Base rate for a company hitting a slipped, uncommitted 'next month' GA target within a specific 11-day sub-window, with a high GA bar: ~15%
input.assessment: Question requires full GA/stable status on the Gemini API pages specifically (not Vertex preview) within a tight 11-day window. As of June 29, the model is only in limited Vertex enterprise preview and absent from the Gemini API docs entirely. Google has already slipped once and won't commit to a date. Even conditional on a July public launch, the standard preview-first pattern means an initial Gemini API listing would likely carry a -preview suffix, which is explicitly excluded. Multiple independent factors point NO. Estimate ~0.10-0.12.
input.calibration_notes: Status quo (NO) is strongly favored: not yet on Gemini API even in preview. Not hedging toward 50% — evidence clearly supports low probability.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether Google does an early-July GA event AND lists it as stable (not preview) on the Gemini API by July 11. Both required.
input.tool_audit: web_search returned rich recent coverage (June 25-29 articles); fetch_url on techtimes confirmed status as of June 29 (still Vertex limited preview, GA expected July, no committed date). No tool failures.
input.process_reflection: Straightforward definitional/predictive question with clear resolution criteria. Web search and one fetch were sufficient; no need for heavy subforecasting. Scaffolding worked smoothly.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
All four factors are grounded in the research trace, with no hallucination, double-counting, or wrong-direction errors.

VERIFICATION:
- Factor 1 (tight 11-day window, −0.9): Supported. Window is June 27–July 11; trace confirms "July 2026" is a loose target with no committed date.
- Factor 2 (already slipped June→July, Google won't comment, −0.7): Directly supported — fetch_url states "Google has declined to comment on the timeline" and multiple search results confirm the slip.
- Factor 3 (still limited Vertex enterprise preview, not on Gemini API pages, preview-first means likely -preview which is excluded, −1.1): The dominant factor and strongly grounded — fetch_url: "As of June 29, 2026, the model remained in limited preview for select Vertex AI enterprise customers." Codersera and Bind articles corroborate internal/limited preview status. The preview-first inference is reasonable.
- Factor 4 (competitive/talent pressure favors early-July launch, +0.6): Grounded (talent exodus to Anthropic, GPT-5.6/Claude competition, "cleared for July launch"). Sign correctly positive toward YES.

RESOLUTION ALIGNMENT: Excellent. The agent correctly distinguishes GA/stable on the two named Gemini API pages from Vertex-only preview (which the criteria explicitly exclude), and correctly flags the -preview suffix exclusion. Factors engage the criteria's specific terms.

PRE-PUBLICATION EVENT: Not applicable — no factor claims the question is already resolved; the model remains in preview.

TOOL AUDIT: Agent's "no tool failures" matches ground truth (3 calls, 0 errors). Accurate.

COUNTERARGUMENT: Strong and genuine — constructs a coherent YES story (I/O commitment, competitive/talent pressure, technical maturity, the Flash-straight-to-GA precedent on May 19, possible early-July event), not a token negation.

PROBABILITY: My independent estimate (8–13%) aligns with the agent's 11%. The departure from the ~15% anchor is modest and justified by Factor 3 plus cumulative timing constraints. Confidence of 0.78 is calibrated given fresh, convergent June 25–29 sourcing.

No worldview entries or historical forecasts exist to cross-check (none found). No errors warranting warn or fail.
